Management Zone Delineation Report

Fuzzy C-Means with PCA dimensionality reduction and ordinary kriging

Author

MZ Analysis

Published

June 17, 2026

1 Executive summary

This report summarizes the delineation of 5 management zones over a study area (Study Area) covering 1,572,337 valid raster cells (out of 4,089,056 total) at the resolution of the input soil-property raster. Zone counts were evaluated over 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and the optimal value k = 5 was selected by the consensus of the Xie-Beni (XB), Fuzzy Performance Index (FPI), and Normalized Classification Entropy (NCE) indices.

The analysis used 99 observation points and 10 soil properties (BD, CEC, Fragm, Sand, Silt, Clay, N, OCD, pH, SOC). Membership surfaces were interpolated by kriging; 88.3% of the study area falls in a transition zone (maximum membership below 0.6).

2 1. Inputs

The pipeline reads three local data sources. Their metadata is summarized below.

Input data summary
Input File Format Description
Study-area boundary agro_geo.gpkg GeoPackage, 1 feature(s) Single polygon defining the crop field extent
Soil-property raster soil_predictions.tif GeoTIFF, 10 layer(s), 4,090,752 cells Multi-layer stack: BD, CEC, Fragm, Sand, Silt, Clay, N, OCD, pH, SOC
Observation points soilgrids_data.csv CSV, 99 rows, 13 cols Columns: fid, longitude, latitude, BD, CEC, Fragm, Sand, Silt, Clay, N, OCD, pH, SOC

2.1 1.1 Coordinate reference system

Points are read in EPSG:4326 (lon/lat) and reprojected to the raster CRS. The boundary is reprojected to the raster CRS before crop and mask. The raster and the boundary must spatially overlap; otherwise the pipeline aborts with a clear error.

2.2 1.2 Study-area boundary

3 2. Pre-processing

3.1 2.1 Raster value extraction at observation points

Raster values were extracted at every observation point using terra::extract (called with the terra:: namespace to avoid a collision with tidyr::extract). Where the raster layer name matched a column in the input CSV, the raster value overwrote the CSV value; otherwise the CSV value was retained.

3.2 2.2 NA imputation and normalization

Any remaining missing values in the soil-property matrix were median-imputed per variable. Features were then min-max normalized to the unit interval [0, 1] to make them commensurate for the PCA and FCM stages.

4 3. Dimensionality reduction (PCA)

PCA was run on the normalized feature matrix (no centering or scaling, since inputs are already in [0, 1]). Principal components were retained until cumulative variance reached 80%.

PCA eigenvalues and variance explained
PC Eigenvalue Proportion Cumulative
1 2.4066 0.8644 0.8644
2 0.1498 0.0538 0.9182
3 0.0775 0.0278 0.9460
4 0.0541 0.0194 0.9654
5 0.0379 0.0136 0.9791
6 0.0218 0.0078 0.9869
7 0.0148 0.0053 0.9922
8 0.0121 0.0043 0.9965
9 0.0077 0.0028 0.9993
10 0.0020 0.0007 1.0000

5 4. Cluster-validity analysis

Fuzzy C-Means was fit on the retained PCA scores for every k in 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 (fuzziness m = 2, iter.max = 300, fixed seed = 42). Five cluster-validity indices were computed:

  • XB — Xie-Beni index (lower is better)
  • FPI — Fuzzy Performance Index (lower is better; McBratney & Moore 1985)
  • NCE — Normalized Classification Entropy (lower is better)
  • PE — Partition Entropy (lower is better)
  • FS — Fukuyama-Sugeno index (lower is better)

The optimal k was selected as the consensus minimum of the XB, FPI, and NCE ranks.

Cluster-validity indices and ranks
k XB FPI NCE PE FS XB_rank FPI_rank NCE_rank consensus_rank
5 0.06231 0.2695 0.2716 0.4372 -107.40 1 2 2 5
6 0.11020 0.2621 0.2523 0.4521 -125.10 5 1 1 7
3 0.09171 0.2948 0.3286 0.3610 -82.69 2 3 4 9
4 0.09621 0.3000 0.3086 0.4278 -97.85 3 4 3 10
2 0.10400 0.3751 0.4379 0.3035 -46.34 4 5 5 14

6 5. Final FCM model and zone map

The final FCM was fit with the optimal k = 5 (consensus rank = 5). Each observation point was assigned to the zone with the largest membership, and the k membership surfaces were interpolated across the study area by kriging.

6.1 5.1 Zone assignment summary

Observation points per zone
Zone Points Share (%)
1 12 12.1
2 37 37.4
3 21 21.2
4 25 25.3
5 4 4.0

6.2 5.2 Transition mask

A cell is flagged as a transition zone when the maximum membership at that cell is below 0.6. 88.3% of the study area falls in this category; the remaining 11.7% is a “core” zone with a confident assignment.

Zone BD CEC Fragm Sand Silt Clay N OCD pH SOC

7.1 6.1 ANOVA and effect size (η²)

For each soil variable, a one-way ANOVA tested the null hypothesis that the variable mean is equal across zones. The η² effect size is reported in addition to the p-value.

Per-variable ANOVA and effect size
Variable η² p-value Sig.
BD 0.068 0.1504000
CEC 0.084 0.0817400 .
Fragm 0.351 0.0000000 ***
Sand 0.117 0.0191300 *
Silt 0.089 0.0649000 .
Clay 0.067 0.1580000
N 0.269 0.0000054 ***
OCD 0.147 0.0044040 **
pH 0.237 0.0000360 ***
SOC 0.047 0.3339000

Note. The η² values above are computed on the original CSV observations for transparency. The per-zone means in the previous table were computed on the post-extraction matrix (raster values overwrite CSV values where available) used during clustering. Differences are small in practice and only affect cells where the CSV had a value and the raster had a different one.

8 7. Uncertainty map (membership entropy)

Fuzzy assignments carry intrinsic uncertainty: a cell near the boundary between two zones will have intermediate memberships for both. The Shannon entropy of the membership vector at each cell summarizes this:

\[H(\mathbf{u}) = -\sum_{j=1}^{k} u_j \log u_j\]

Higher entropy ⇒ more ambiguous assignment. This is a useful diagnostic, since transition zones defined by a hard threshold (Section 5.2) lose the gradient information.

Entropy distribution
Statistic Value
Min 0.2540
1st quartile 1.2347
Median 1.3181
Mean 1.3515
3rd quartile 1.2783
Max 1.4988
log(k) ceiling 1.6094

9 8. Reproducibility

Pipeline parameters
Parameter Value
Fuzziness (m) 2
k range 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
PCA cumulative-variance threshold 0.8
Transition threshold (max membership) 0.6
Interpolation method kriging
Random seed 42
